author: Augustine of Hippo
book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.

ccel.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

title: Psalm VI

source.title: NPNF1-08. St. Augustine: Exposition on the Book of Psalms

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: To the end, in the hymns of the eighth, a psalm to David.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Of the eighth,” seems here obscure.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the rest of this title is more clear.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now it has seemed to some to intimate the day of judgment, that is, the time of the coming of our Lord, when He will come to judge the quick and dead.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Which coming, it is believed, is to be, after reckoning the years from Adam, seven thousand years: so as that seven thousand years should pass as seven days, and afterwards that time arrive as it were the eighth day.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But since it has been said by the Lord, “It is not yours to know the times, which the Father hath put in His own power:” and, “But of the day and that hour knoweth no man, no, neither angel, nor Power, neither the Son, but the Father alone:” and again, that which is written, “that the day of the Lord cometh as a thief,” shows clearly enough that no man should arrogate to himself the knowledge of that time, by any computation of years.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For if that day is to come after seven thousand years, every man could learn its advent by reckoning the years.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What comes then of the Son’s even not knowing this?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Which of course is said with this meaning, that men do not learn this by the Son, not that He by Himself doth not know it: according to that form of speech, “the Lord your God trieth you that He may know;” that is, that He may make you know: and, “arise, O Lord;” that is, make us arise.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: When therefore the Son is thus said not to know this day; not because He knoweth it not, but because He causeth those to know it not, for whom it is not expedient to know it, that is, He doth not show it to them; what does that strange presumption mean, which, by a reckoning up of years, expects the day of the Lord as most certain after seven thousand years?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Be we then willingly ignorant of that which the Lord would not have us know: and let us inquire what this title, “of the eighth,” means.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The day of judgment may indeed, even without any rash computation of years, be understood by the eighth, for that immediately after the end of this world, life eternal being attained, the souls of the righteous will not then be subject unto times: and, since all times have their revolution in a repetition of those seven days, that peradventure is called the eighth day, which will not have this variety.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: There is another reason, which may be here not unreasonably accepted, why the judgment should be called the eighth, because it will take place after two generations, one relating to the body, the other to the soul.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For from Adam unto Moses the human race lived of the body, that is, according to the flesh: which is called the outward and the old man, and to which the Old Testament was given, that it might prefigure the spiritual things to come by operations, albeit religious, yet carnal.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Through this entire season, when men lived according to the body, “death reigned,” as the Apostle saith, “even over those that had not sinned.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now it reigned “after the similitude of Adam’s transgression,” as the same Apostle saith; for it must be taken of the period up to Moses, up to which time the works of the law, that is, those sacraments of carnal observance, held even those bound, for the sake of a certain mystery, who were subject to the One God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But from the coming of the Lord, from whom there was a transition from the circumcision of the flesh to the circumcision of the heart, the call was made, that man should live according to the soul, that is, according to the inner man, who is also called the “new man” by reason of the new birth and the renewing of spiritual conversation.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now it is plain that the number four has relation to the body, from the four well known elements of which it consists, and the four qualities of dry, humid, warm, cold.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hence too it is administered by four seasons, spring, summer, autumn, winter.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: All this is very well known.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For of the number four relating to the body we have treated elsewhere somewhat subtilly, but obscurely: which must be avoided in this discourse, which we would have accommodated to the unlearned.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But that the number three has relation to the mind may be understood from this, that we are commanded to love God after a threefold manner, with the whole heart, with the whole soul, with the whole mind: of each of which severally we must treat, not in the Psalms, but in the Gospels: for the present, for proof of the relation of the number three to the mind, I think what has been said enough.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Those numbers then of the body which have relation to the old man and the Old Testament, being past and gone, the numbers too of the soul, which have relation to the new man and the New Testament, being past and gone, a septenary so to say being passed; because everything is done in time, four having been distributed to the body, three to the mind; the eighth will come, the day of judgment: which assigning to deserts their due, will transfer at once the saint, not to temporal works, but to eternal life; but will condemn the ungodly to eternal punishment.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In fear of which condemnation the Church prays in this Psalm, and says, “Reprove me not, O Lord, in Thine anger” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The Apostle too mentions the anger of the judgment; “Thou treasurest up unto thyself,” he says, “anger against the day of the anger of the just judgment of God.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In which he would not be reproved, whosoever longs to be healed in this life.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Nor in Thy rage chasten me.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Chasten,” seems rather too mild a word; for it availeth toward amendment.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For for him who is reproved, that is, accused, it is to be feared lest his end be condemnation.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But since “rage” seems to be more than “anger,” it may be a difficulty, why that which is milder, namely, chastening, is joined to that which is more severe, namely, rage.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But I suppose that one and the same thing is signified by the two words.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For in the Greek θυμὸς, which is in the first verse, means the same as ὀργὴ, which is in the second verse.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But when the Latins themselves too wished to use two distinct words, they looked out for what was akin to “anger,” and “rage” was used.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hence copies vary.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For in some “anger” is found first, and then “rage:” in others, for “rage,” “indignation” or “choler” is used.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But whatever the reading, it is an emotion of the soul urging to the infliction of punishment.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Yet this emotion must not be attributed to God, as if to a soul, of whom it is said, “but Thou, O Lord of power, judgest with tranquillity.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now that which is tranquil, is not disturbed.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Disturbance then does not attach to God as judge: but what is done by His ministers, in that it is done by His laws, is called His anger.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In which anger, the soul, which now prays, would not only not be reproved, but not even chastened, that is, amended or instructed.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For in the Greek it is, παιδεύσῃς, that is, instruct.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now in the day of judgment all are “reproved” that hold not the foundation, which is Christ.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But they are amended, that is, purged, who “upon this foundation build wood, hay, stubble.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For they shall suffer loss, but shall be saved, as by fire.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What then does he pray, who would not be either reproved or amended in the anger of the Lord? what else but that he may be healed?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For where sound health is, neither death is to be dreaded, nor the physician’s hand with caustics or the knife.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He proceeds accordingly to say, “Pity me, O Lord, for I am weak: heal me, O Lord, for my bones are troubled” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 2), that is, the support of my soul, or strength: for this is the meaning of “bones.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The soul therefore says, that her strength is troubled, when she speaks of bones.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For it is not to be supposed, that the soul has bones, such as we see in the body.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Wherefore, what follows tends to explain it, “and my soul is troubled exceedingly” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 3), lest because he mentioned bones, they should be understood as of the body.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “And Thou, O Lord, how long?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Who does not see represented here a soul struggling with her diseases; but long kept back by the physician, that she may be convinced what evils she has plunged herself into through sin?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For what is easily healed, is not much avoided: but from the difficulty of the healing, there will be the more careful keeping of recovered health.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: God then, to whom it is said, “And Thou, O Lord, how long?” must not be deemed as if cruel: but as a kind convincer of the soul, what evil she hath procured for herself.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For this soul does not yet pray so perfectly, as that it can be said to her, “Whilst thou art yet speaking I will say, Behold, here I am.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: That she may at the same time also come to know, if they who do turn meet with so great difficulty, how great punishment is prepared for the ungodly, who will not turn to God: as it is written in another place, “If the righteous scarcely be saved, where shall the sinner and ungodly appear?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Turn, O Lord, and deliver my soul” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Turning herself she prays that God too would turn to her: as it is said, “Turn ye unto Me, and I will turn unto you, saith the Lord.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Or is it to be understood according to that way of speaking, “Turn, O Lord,” that is make me turn, since the soul in this her turning feels difficulty and toil?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For our perfected turning findeth God ready, as says the Prophet, “We shall find Him ready as the dawn.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Since it was not His absence who is everywhere present, but our turning away that made us lose Him; “He was in this world,” it is said, “and the world was made by Him, and the world knew Him not.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If, then, He was in this world, and the world knew Him not, our impurity doth not endure the sight of Him.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But whilst we are turning ourselves, that is, by changing our old life are fashioning our spirit; we feel it hard and toilsome to be wrested back from the darkness of earthly lusts, to the serene and quiet and tranquillity of the divine light.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And in such difficulty we say, “Turn, O Lord,” that is, help us, that that turning may be perfected in us, which findeth Thee ready, and offering Thyself for the fruition of them that love Thee.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And hence after he said, “Turn, O Lord,” he added, “and deliver my soul:” cleaving as it were to the entanglements of this world, and suffering, in the very act of turning, from the thorns, as it were, of rending and tearing desires.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Make me whole,” he says, “for Thy pity’s sake.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He knows that it is not of his own merits that he is healed: for to him sinning, and transgressing a given command, was just condemnation due.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Heal me therefore, he says, not for my merit’s sake, but for Thy pity’s sake.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For in death there is no one that is mindful of Thee” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He knows too that now is the time for turning unto God: for when this life shall have passed away, there remaineth but a retribution of our deserts.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “But in hell who shall confess to Thee?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: That rich man, of whom the Lord speaks, who saw Lazarus in rest, but bewailed himself in torments, confessed in hell, yea so as to wish even to have his brethren warned, that they might keep themselves from sin, because of the punishment which is not believed to be in hell.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Although therefore to no purpose, yet he confessed that those torments had deservedly lighted upon him; since he even wished his brethren to be instructed, lest they should fall into the same.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What then is, “But in hell who will confess to Thee?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Is hell to be understood as that place, whither the ungodly will be cast down after the judgment, when by reason of that deeper darkness they will no more see any light of God, to whom they may confess aught?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For as yet that rich man by raising his eyes, although a vast gulf lay between, could still see Lazarus established in rest: by comparing himself with whom, he was driven to a confession of his own deserts.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It may be understood also, as if the Psalmist calls sin, that is committed in contempt of God’s law, death: so as that we should give the name of death to the sting of death, because it procures death.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For the sting of death is sin.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In which death this is to be unmindful of God, to despise His law and commandments: so that by hell the Psalmist would mean that blindness of soul which overtakes and enwraps the sinner, that is, the dying.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “As they did not think good,” the Apostle says, “to retain God in” their “knowledge, God gave them over to a reprobate mind.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: From this death, and this hell, the soul earnestly prays that she may be kept safe, whilst she strives to turn to God, and feels her difficulties.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Wherefore he goes on to say, “I have laboured in my groaning.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And as if this availed but little, he adds, “I will wash each night my couch” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: That is here called a couch, where the sick and weak soul rests, that is, in bodily gratification and in every worldly pleasure.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Which pleasure, whoso endeavours to withdraw himself from it, washes with tears.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For he sees that he already condemns carnal lusts; and yet his weakness is held by the pleasure, and willingly lies down therein, from whence none but the soul that is made whole can rise.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: As for what he says, “each night,” he would perhaps have it taken thus: that he who, ready in spirit, perceives some light of truth, and yet, through weakness of the flesh, rests sometime in the pleasure of this world, is compelled to suffer as it were days and nights in an alternation of feeling: as when he says, “With the mind I serve the law of God,” he feels as it were day; again when he says, “but with the flesh the law of sin,” he declines into night: until all night passeth away, and that one day comes, of which it is said, “In the morning I will stand by Thee, and will see.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For then he will stand, but now he lies down, when he is on his couch; which he will wash each night, that with so great abundance of tears he may obtain the most assured remedy from the mercy of God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “I will drench my bed with tears.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is a repetition.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For when he says, “with tears,” he shows with what meaning he said above, “I will wash.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For we take “bed” here to be the same as “couch” above.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Although, “I will drench,” is something more than, “I will wash:” since anything may be washed superficially, but drenching penetrates to the more inward parts; which here signifies weeping to the very bottom of the heart.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now the variety of tenses which he uses; the past, when he said, “I have laboured in my groaning;” and the future, when he said, “I will wash each night my couch;” the future again, “I will drench my bed with tears;” this shows what every man ought to say to himself, when he labours in groaning to no purpose.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: As if he should say, It hath not profited when I have done this, therefore I will do the other.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Mine eye is disordered by anger” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: 7): is it by his own, or God’s anger, in which he maketh petition that he might not be reproved, or chastened?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But if anger in that place intimate the day of judgment, how can it be understood now?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Is it a beginning of it, that men here suffer pains and torments, and above all the loss of the understanding of the truth; as I have already quoted that which is said, “God gave them over to a reprobate mind”?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For such is the blindness of the mind.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Whosoever is given over thereunto, is shut out from the interior light of God: but not wholly as yet, whilst he is in this life.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For there is “outer darkness,” which is understood to belong rather to the day of judgment; that he should rather be wholly without God, whosoever whilst there is time refuses correction.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now to be wholly without God, what else is it, but to be in extreme blindness?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: If indeed God “dwell in inaccessible light,” whereinto they enter, to whom it is said, “Enter thou into the joy of thy Lord.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is then the beginning of this anger, which in this life every sinner suffers.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: In fear therefore of the day of judgment, he is in trial and grief; lest he be brought to that, the disastrous commencement of which he experiences now.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And therefore he did not say, mine eye is extinguished, but, “mine eye is disordered by anger.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But if he mean that his eye is disordered by his own anger, there is no wonder either in this.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For hence perhaps it is said, “Let not the sun go down upon your wrath;” because the mind, which, from her own disorder, is not permitted to see God, supposes that the inner sun, that is, the wisdom of God, suffers as it were a setting in her.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “I have grown old in all mine enemies.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He had only spoken of anger (if it were yet of his own anger that he spoke): but thinking on his other vices, he found that he was entrenched by them all.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Which vices, as they belong to the old life and the old man, which we must put off, that we may put on the new man, it is well said, “I have grown old.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But “in all mine enemies,” he means, either amidst these vices, or amidst men who will not be converted to God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For these, even if they know them not, even if they bear with them, even if they use the same tables and houses and cities, with no strife arising between them, and in frequent converse together with seeming concord: notwithstanding, by the contrariety of their aims, they are enemies to those who turn unto God.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For seeing that the one love and desire this world, the others wish to be freed from this world, who sees not that the first are enemies to the last?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For if they can, they draw the others into punishment with them.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And it is a great grace, to be conversant daily with their words, and not to depart from the way of God’s commandments.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For often the mind which is striving to go on to God-ward, being rudely handled in the very road, is alarmed; and generally fulfils not its good intent, lest it should offend those with whom it lives, who love and follow after other perishable and transient goods.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: From such every one that is whole is separated, not in space, but in soul.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For the body is contained in space, but the soul’s space is her affection.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Wherefore after the labour, and groaning, and very frequent showers of tears, since that cannot be ineffectual, which is asked so earnestly of Him, who is the Fountain of all mercies, and it is most truly said, “the Lord is nigh unto them that are of a broken heart:” after difficulties so great, the pious soul, by which we may also understand the Church, intimating that she has been heard, see what she adds: “Depart from me, all ye that work iniquity; for the Lord hath heard the voice of my weeping” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is either spoken prophetically, since they will depart, that is, the ungodly will be separated from the righteous, when the day of judgment arrives, or, for this time present.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For although both are equally found in the same assemblies, yet on the open floor the wheat is already separated from the chaff, though it be hid among the chaff.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: They can therefore be associated together, but cannot be carried away by the wind together.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “For the Lord hath heard the voice of my weeping; The Lord hath heard my supplication; the Lord hath received my prayer” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The frequent repetition of the same sentiments shows not, so to say, the necessities of the narrator, but the warm feeling of his joy.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For they that rejoice are wont so to speak, as that it is not enough for them to declare once for all the object of their joy.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: This is the fruit of that groaning in which there is labour, and those tears with which the couch is washed, and bed drenched: for, “he that sows in tears, shall reap in joy:” and, “blessed are they that mourn, for they shall be comforted.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: “Let all mine enemies be ashamed and vexed” (ver.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: He said above, “depart from me all ye:” which can take place, as it has been explained, even in this life: but as to what he says, “let them be ashamed and vexed,” I do not see how it can happen, save on that day when the rewards of the righteous and the punishments of the sinners shall be made manifest.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For at present so far are the ungodly from being ashamed, that they do not cease to insult us.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: And for the most part their mockings are of such avail, that they make the weak to be ashamed of the name of Christ.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Hence it is said, “Whosoever shall be ashamed of Me before men, of him will I be ashamed before My Father.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But now whosoever would fulfil those sublime commands, to disperse, to give to the poor, that his righteousness may endure for ever; and selling all his earthly goods, and spending them on the needy, would follow Christ, saying, “We brought nothing into this world, and truly we can carry nothing out; having food and raiment, let us be therewith content;” incurs the profane raillery of those men, and by those who will not be made whole, is called mad; and often to avoid being so called by desperate men, he fears to do, and puts off that, which the most faithful and powerful of all physicians hath ordered.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: It is not then at present that these can be ashamed, by whom we have to wish that we be not made ashamed, and so be either called back from our proposed journey, or hindered, or delayed.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But the time will come when they shall be ashamed, saying as it is written, “These are they whom we had sometimes in derision, and a parable of reproach: we fools counted their life madness, and their end to be without honour: how are they numbered among the children of God, and their lot is among the saints?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Therefore have we erred from the way of truth, and the light of righteousness hath not shined into us, nor the sun risen upon us: we have been filled with the way of wickedness and destruction, and have walked through rugged deserts, but the way of the Lord we have not known.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: What hath pride profited us, or what hath the vaunting of riches brought us?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: All those things are passed away like a shadow.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But as to what he says, “Let them be turned and confounded,” who would not judge it to be a most righteous punishment, that they should have a turning unto confusion, who would not have one unto salvation?

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: After this he added, “exceeding quickly.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For when the day of judgment shall have begun to be no longer looked for, when they shall have said, “Peace, then shall sudden destruction come upon them.”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Now whensoever it come, that comes very quickly, of whose coming we give up all expectation; and nothing makes the length of this life be felt but the hope of living.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: For nothing seems more quick, than all that has already passed in it.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: When then the day of judgment shall come, then will sinners feel how that all the life which passeth away is not long.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Nor will that any way possibly seem to them to have come tardily, which shall have come without their desiring, or rather without their believing.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: Although it can too be taken in this place thus, that inasmuch as God has heard, so to say, her groans, and her long and frequent tears, she may be understood to be freed from her sins, and to have tamed every disordered impulse of carnal affection: as she saith, “Depart from me, all ye that work iniquity, for the Lord hath heard the voice of my weeping:” and when she has had this happy issue, it is no marvel if she be already so perfect as to pray for her enemies.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: The words then, “Let all mine enemies be ashamed, and vexed,” may have this meaning; that they should repent of their sins, which cannot be effected without confusion and vexation.

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: There is then nothing to hinder us from taking what follows too in this sense, “let them be turned and ashamed,” that is, let them be turned to God, and be ashamed that they sometime gloried in the former darkness of their sins; as the Apostle says, “For what glory had ye sometime in those things of which ye are now ashamed?”

verses.book: Expositions on the Book of Psalms.
verses.text: But as to what he added, “exceeding quickly,” it must be referred either to the warm affection of her wish, or to the power of Christ; who converteth to the faith of the Gospel in such quick time the nations, which in their idols’ cause did persecute the Church.
